title: The Complexity Tax: Why More Product Lines Rarely Means More Profit
linkedin:
Revenue up. Profit flat. It is one of the most common patterns we see in owner-led businesses, and it is rarely caused by anything dramatic.

It is caused by range creep. A new service added to answer a customer request. A premium tier. A smaller pack size or a bespoke variant for one big client. Each addition looked sensible on its own.

A few years of that and most businesses are carrying a much wider range than they realise, and a large share of it is not actually earning its keep. In our experience, a small number of lines typically generate most of the real profit, while a much longer tail adds revenue without adding margin once the true cost of serving it, the stock, the admin, the staff time split across more things, is counted properly.

None of this shows up clearly at the top of the P&L. Total revenue and even total gross margin can look perfectly healthy while the mix underneath quietly shifts against you.

The fix is not complicated once the numbers are visible. Every line gets a kill, fix or scale decision: invest further in the ones quietly carrying the business, adjust the ones that could earn their place with a price or delivery change, and retire the ones consuming resources that would generate more profit elsewhere.

If your revenue has grown steadily but your profit has not kept pace, the range you offer is one of the first places worth checking, and one of the few improvements that does not require winning a single new customer.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #SMEFinance #BusinessOwners
facebook:
Quick one for the business owners here: has your revenue grown over the last year or two while your profit has barely moved?

We see this constantly, and it is almost never one big problem. It is a slow build-up of extra products, services or variants added one reasonable decision at a time, until the range is wider than the business can really support without diluting attention and margin.

The interesting part is that a small handful of lines usually carry most of the real profit, while a longer tail is quietly costing more to run than it earns.

Have you ever actually ranked your products or services by what they truly contribute, rather than by revenue?

#SMEFinance
google:
A note for local business owners as the new financial year gets underway. One pattern shows up again and again in owner-led businesses across Brisbane, Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria: revenue climbs steadily year after year, but profit does not follow at the same pace.

More often than not, the cause is a product or service range that has quietly grown wider over time. A new service here, a premium option there, a small variant added for one client. Each addition made sense at the time, but together they add stock holding costs, admin hours and stretched attention that rarely show up as a clear line in the accounts.

The useful exercise is ranking every product or service by what it genuinely contributes once its real costs are counted, not by how much revenue it brings in. In our experience, a concentrated group of lines usually carries most of the profit, while a longer tail adds little once properly measured.

If your revenue has been growing but your profit has not kept up, this is one of the first places worth looking, and one of the few improvements that does not depend on winning a single new customer. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
